title: Behold the First Realistic Depiction of the Human Face (Circa 25,000 BCE)
description:
in Art, History |   	March 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

?si=HOvgnTtB4xSNqpNE
In 1894, archae┬Łol┬Ło┬Łgist ├ēdouard Piette dis┬Łcov┬Łered the ŌĆ£Venu
body:
in Art, History |   	March 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

?si=HOvgnTtB4xSNqpNE
In 1894, archae┬Łol┬Ło┬Łgist ├ēdouard Piette dis┬Łcov┬Łered the ŌĆ£Venus of Brassem┬Łpouy,ŌĆØ oth┬Łer┬Łwise known as the ŌĆ£Lady with the Hood.ŌĆØ Unearthed in south┬Łwest┬Łern France and dat┬Łing to around 25,000┬ĀBCE, this carv┬Łing rep┬Łre┬Łsents the ear┬Łli┬Łest real┬Łis┬Łtic depic┬Łtion of a human face. The figureŌĆÖs fore┬Łhead, nose, and brows are care┬Łful┬Łly carved in relief, as is the hair, arranged in a neat geo┬Łmet┬Łric pat┬Łtern. But what hap┬Łpened to the mouth? Or the eyes? WeŌĆÖre not sure.
The Venus is carved from mam┬Łmoth ivory, like┬Łly using a stone flint, and stands just 3.65┬Ācm tall. For some, it marks a major devel┬Łop┬Łment in fig┬Łu┬Łra┬Łtive art. Or, as his┬Łto┬Łri┬Łan Simon Schama has sug┬Łgest┬Łed, this fig┬Łurine may well be the ŌĆ£dawn of the idea of beau┬ŁtyŌĆØ in human cul┬Łture.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent┬Ā
The WorldŌĆÖs Old┬Łest Cave Art, Dis┬Łcov┬Łered in Indone┬Łsia, Is at Least 67,800 Years Old
Alger┬Łian Cave Paint┬Łings Sug┬Łgest Humans Did Mag┬Łic Mush┬Łrooms 9,000 Years Ago
Was a 32,000-Year-Old Cave Paint┬Łing the Ear┬Łli┬Łest Form of Cin┬Łe┬Łma?
A Styl┬Łish 2,000-Year-Old Roman Shoe Found in a Well
Exquis┬Łite 2300-Year-Old Scythi┬Łan WomanŌĆÖs Boot Pre┬Łserved in the Frozen Ground of Siberia

title: The Greatest Double Agent Ever: How a Spanish Chicken Farmer Became the Most Important Double Agent in WWII
description:
in History |   	March 2nd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Juan Pujol Gar┬Łc├Ła was one of the rare indi┬Łvid┬Łu┬Łals whose par┬Łtic┬Łi┬Łpa┬Łtion in World War II m
body:
in History |   	March 2nd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Juan Pujol Gar┬Łc├Ła was one of the rare indi┬Łvid┬Łu┬Łals whose par┬Łtic┬Łi┬Łpa┬Łtion in World War II made him a Mem┬Łber of the Order of the British Empire and earned him the Iron Cross. He gained that unlike┬Łly dis┬Łtinc┬Łtion in per┬Łhaps the riski┬Łest of all roles in espi┬Łonage, that of a dou┬Łble agent. Despite ulti┬Łmate┬Łly work┬Łing for the Allied cause, he cre┬Łat┬Łed an elab┬Ło┬Łrate fic┬Łtion┬Łal per┬Łsona ŌĆö com┬Łplete with an invent┬Łed spy net┬Łwork oper┬Łat┬Łing across Great Britain ŌĆö who pro┬Łfessed loy┬Łal┬Łty to the Nazi cause. Not only did Pujol get this char┬Łac┬Łter plugged into the real Ger┬Łman intel┬Łli┬Łgence sys┬Łtem, he also got him on its pay┬Łroll, receiv┬Łing what came to the equiv┬Ła┬Łlent of more than $6 mil┬Łlion in todayŌĆÖs U.S. dol┬Łlars for sup┬Łply┬Łing infor┬Łma┬Łtion ŌĆö infor┬Łma┬Łtion that ulti┬Łmate┬Łly con┬Łtributed to the AxisŌĆÖ loss of the war.
The sto┬Łry of how this chick┬Łen farmer from Barcelona became the most impor┬Łtant dou┬Łble agent of World War II is told in the ani┬Łmat┬Łed Pri┬Łmal Space video above. Unlike many of the spies his┬Łto┬Łry has remem┬Łbered more clear┬Łly, Pujol did┬ŁnŌĆÖt begin his espi┬Łonage career in the employ of any gov┬Łern┬Łment in par┬Łtic┬Łu┬Łlar.
Rad┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łized, if that be the word, by the expe┬Łri┬Łence of hav┬Łing been draft┬Łed into the Span┬Łish Civ┬Łil War, he vowed to ded┬Łi┬Łcate his life to ŌĆ£the good of human┬Łi┬Łty.ŌĆØ Turned away by the British embassy, to which heŌĆÖd offered his ser┬Łvices because Britain opposed Nazi Ger┬Łmany, he went free┬Łlance, re-invent┬Łing him┬Łself as a Third Reich-loy┬Łal Span┬Łish mil┬Łi┬Łtary man seek┬Łing an assign┬Łment in the U.K. Tak┬Łen on by Ger┬Łmany, he instead decamped to Lis┬Łbon, where he began man┬Łu┬Łfac┬Łtur┬Łing ersatz intel┬Łli┬Łgence reports using news┬Łreel footage and tourist brochures.
How┬Łev┬Łer makeshift, PujolŌĆÖs craft proved impres┬Łsive to both Ger┬Łmany and Britain, which launched an inter┬Łna┬Łtion┬Łal spy hunt for him. He thus accom┬Łplished his goal of becom┬Łing an offi┬Łcial British dou┬Łble agent, in which capac┬Łi┬Łty he arrived at his finest hour: mis┬Łlead┬Łing the Ger┬Łmans as to the 1944 ŌĆ£DŌĆæDayŌĆØ inva┬Łsion of Nor┬Łmandy in an effort called Oper┬Ła┬Łtion For┬Łti┬Łtude. In Span┬Łish, that would be For┬Łt┬Łaleza, which became the title of an RTVE doc┬Łu┬Łmen┬Łtary about PujolŌĆÖs long-untold sto┬Łry a few years ago. But if any sin┬Łgle word reflects PujolŌĆÖs con┬Łtri┬Łbu┬Łtion to his┬Łto┬Łry, that word must be Gar┬Łbo, the code name assigned him by his first British case offi┬Łcer. After all, what oth┬Łer name ŌĆö at least in 1942 ŌĆö could quite so evoca┬Łtive┬Łly befit an agent whose skills of craft┬Łing and inhab┬Łit┬Łing invent┬Łed char┬Łac┬Łters made his han┬Łdlers regard him as ŌĆ£the best actor in the worldŌĆØ?
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
The CIAŌĆÖs For┬Łmer Chief of Dis┬Łguise Show How Spies Use Cos┬Łtumes in Under┬Łcov┬Łer Oper┬Ła┬Łtions
The Sto┬Łry of Elize┬Łbeth Fried┬Łman, the Pio┬Łneer┬Łing Cryp┬Łtol┬Ło┬Łgist Who Thwart┬Łed the Nazis & Got Burned by J. Edgar Hoover
The French Designed a Fake Repli┬Łca of Paris to Fool Ger┬Łman Bombers Dur┬Łing World War I
Dis┬Łcov┬Łer the CIAŌĆÖs Sim┬Łple Sab┬Ło┬Łtage Field Man┬Łu┬Łal: A Time┬Łless Guide to Sub┬Łvert┬Łing Any Orga┬Łni┬Łza┬Łtion with ŌĆ£Pur┬Łpose┬Łful Stu┬Łpid┬Łi┬ŁtyŌĆØ (1944)
The CIAŌĆÖs Rec┬Łtal Tool Kit for Spies ŌĆö Cre┬Łat┬Łed for Tru┬Łly Des┬Łper┬Łate Sit┬Łu┬Ła┬Łtions Dur┬Łing The Cold War
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Radioposter Launches Paper-fi: Analog Books with Synchronized Soundtracks
description: For all the talk about 2026 being the year of analog, most of the conversation has been about rediscovering old formats like vinyl, film photograp
body:
For all the talk about 2026 being the year of analog, most of the conversation has been about rediscovering old formats like vinyl, film photography, and journaling. WhatŌĆÖs been missing from these hobbies is something genuinely new, a reason to believe paper can do more than it did before. Radioposter is betting it can.┬Ā
The Midwest-based startup has built what it calls Paper-fi: physical books with synchronized audio soundtracks that follow readers in real time as they turn each page. No chips embedded in the paper, no QR codes to scan. The system uses patented computer vision and other modes through a smartphone or smart glasses to track your place in the book and play the corresponding audio. Whether music, ambient sound, or narration, the soundtrack moves with you at your own pace. The books themselves are designed more like art objects than traditional reading material, showcasing highly visual stories where audio and imagery carry the narrative together. Think coffee table book meets documentary or movie score.┬Ā

Sound design, composition, illustration, photography, and storytelling all collapse into a single physical object. ItŌĆÖs not an audiobook. ItŌĆÖs not a picture book with a playlist. ItŌĆÖs a format that didnŌĆÖt exist before, one in which image, sequence, and sound interact on paper in ways that screens have made us forget.
After years of infinite scroll and algorithmically generated content, people are looking for more tangible hobbies. The appetite for objects you can hold, experiences that have edges and endings, is real. Radioposter is positioning Paper-fi as a way to give the medium new superpowers, making analog competitive again without abandoning what makes internet storytelling compelling.
The company publishes its own titles and the app that makes it all work. Two books have launched already, with more in development. They are also actively looking for artists who want to pitch their work for the format and have programs to support publishing end-to-end.
Whether Paper-fi becomes a lasting medium or a beautiful experiment, it represents something rare: a new analog artifact built for this moment, not borrowed from the past.
Learn more at radioposter.com.

schema.headline: Radioposter Launches Paper-fi: Analog Books with Synchronized Soundtracks

title: Maximum's Upcycled 'Billex' Process Turns Discarded Bank Notes into Furniture
description: Money manufacturers the world over are forever contending with counterfeiters. Before the U.K. introduced a new pound coin in 2017, for example, t
body:
Money manufacturers the world over are forever contending with counterfeiters. Before the U.K. introduced a new pound coin in 2017, for example, the earlier version was easy enough to fake that there were tens of millions of fraudulent copies in circulation. The same goes for paper bank notes, which over the years have been printed with increasingly high-tech features such as holograms, watermarks, and distinctive material blends. More recently, many countries have also implemented plastic coatings.
Banque de France, a central money-producing outfit in Europe, has adopted a technology called EverFit, which includes a polymer coating on cotton-blend notes that increases durability. Every year, up to three billion notes may be printed, but they must adhere to the strictest standards of technical quality. If they donŌĆÖt, theyŌĆÖre shredded. One drawback, though, is that theyŌĆÖre not recyclable. ThatŌĆÖs where Paris-based furniture design studio Maximum saw a unique opportunity.

MaximumŌĆÖs unique line of stools, called Billex, repurposes the masses of discarded bills into modern, functional objects. With a cotton substrate and two layers of plastic coating, the tiny fragments can be manipulated with heat and compressed into a hard shape. Not only is the stool a useful and stylish design piece unto itself, produced in a variety of colors, but the Billex concept is also something of a prototype. It can be employed for a wide range of applications, as the shredded notesŌĆöand the stools themselvesŌĆöcan be upcycled and compressed into virtually any shape.
See more on MaximumŌĆÖs Instagram.

schema.headline: Maximum's Upcycled 'Billex' Process Turns Discarded Bank Notes into Furniture

title: Curator Diya Vij Named NYC Culture Commissioner
description:
News
								The veteran art worker is a ŌĆ£visionary and deeply thoughtful leader,ŌĆØ  said Mayor Mamdani.
body:
News
								The veteran art worker is a ŌĆ£visionary and deeply thoughtful leader,ŌĆØ  said Mayor Mamdani.
						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Curator Diya Vij (photo by Sam Richardson)
    
		
				
						Diya Vij, vice president of curatorial and arts programs at the Brooklyn nonprofit Powerhouse Arts, will be New York City's next culture commissioner.  Rumors about Vij's pick for the top job at the Department of Cultural Affairs (DCLA) have circulated in the local art community for weeks, until the New York Times broke the news today, February 28.Vij, who was a member of Mayor Zohran Mamdani's transition team, will succeed Laurie Cumbo just four months after joining Powerhouse Arts. ŌĆ£This administration has renewed my belief that city government can be a site of real change ŌĆö and that art and culture are essential to that project,ŌĆØ said┬ĀVij in a statement today.┬ĀŌĆ£Too many artists have been forced out of the city they love ŌĆö crushed by the cost-of-living crisis. As Commissioner, I will extend the MayorŌĆÖs affordability agenda to arts and culture. It is an honor to help build a city where artists, cultural workers, and New Yorkers across all five boroughs can do more than get by ŌĆö they can live full, vibrant and curious lives.ŌĆØAn experienced curator with a community-forward approach, she has held several key positions at Creative Time, the High Line, and elsewhere. She has even worked at the DCLA from 2014 to 2019.  Vij got her start as a curatorial fellow at the Queens Museum in 2010, where she worked under the leadership of Tom Finkelpearl, who later served as culture commissioner under Mayor Bill de Blasio.ŌĆ£I feel grateful all the time that it was my entry point into the art world because it was always about understanding how art and community live together and having a blurry understanding of the limits of the fields of art,ŌĆØ she told Hyperallergic in a 2021 podcast interview. ŌĆ£I'm always working around the assumption, idea, and understanding that art is everywhere," she added. "The artists are everywhere...they're in and out of different industries and movement spaces, and part of coalition building.ŌĆØWhen Finkelpearl was tapped as commissioner in 2014, he brought Vij with him. Together with the department's then-Chief of Staff Shirley Levy, she launched the Public Artists in Residence (PAIR) program in 2015, nestling artists within city agencies for a year-long residency to ŌĆ£propose and implement creative solutions to pressing civic challenges.ŌĆØCreative TimeŌĆÖs Diya Vij Helps Launch an Art World Think TankThe unorthodox program will gather together a group of thought leaders for 10 months to reflect and advise on happenings in the art community.HyperallergicHrag VartanianAt Creative Time, Vij spearheaded the organization's CTHQ ŌĆö a permanent event space in Manhattan's Lower East Side that functions as a collaborative hub for artists and activists working in social, economic, and accessibility justice. In that same vein, she launched the organization's Research & Development Fellowship for socially engaged artists and re-ignited the Creative Time Summit in 2024 after a five-year hiatus. Among other major projects, Vij facilitated the Indigenous-led public exhibition The WorldŌĆÖs UnFair (2023) and organized Moving Chains: Toward Abolition (2023), a critical day-long program on Governor's Island.In a statement to the Times, Mayor┬ĀMamdani called Vij a ŌĆ£visionary and deeply thoughtful leader who understands that art is not ornamental to this city ŌĆö it is essential to it.ŌĆØHe continued: ŌĆ£Under DiyaŌĆÖs leadership, we will fight to keep New York a city where artists can afford to live and create, and where every New Yorker, in every borough, can experience the energy and inspiration that makes art possible.ŌĆØ
									
											
												News
											
											
												Diya Vij
											
											
												New York
											
											
												New York City Department of Cultural Affairs

schema.headline: Curator Diya Vij Named NYC Culture Commissioner

title: NYCŌĆÖs New Culture Commissioner
description:
Daily Newsletter
                Diya Vij appointed DCLA commissioner, dispatches from Frieze LA, finding god at the Brooklyn Mu
body:
Daily Newsletter
                Diya Vij appointed DCLA commissioner, dispatches from Frieze LA, finding god at the Brooklyn Museum, and the jazz photos the FBI censored. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced his pick for culture commissioner this weekend. He chose well: Diya Vij, a veteran curator with a strong record of community-forward work, has her heart in the right place. Best of luck to her. Also, please join me today at 3pm (ET) for a virtual conversation with artist-writer Damien Davis. Since he started writing for us in 2024, Damien has emerged as one of the strongest voices in the field. WeŌĆÖll discuss his recent essays, studio practice, and how the two converge. The event is for Hyperallergic Members only. It's not too late to sign up.ŌĆöHakim Bishara, editor-in-chief 
Curator Diya Vij (photo by Sam Richardson)Curator Diya Vij Named NYC Culture CommissionerDiya Vij, who was a member of┬ĀMayor Zohran MamdaniŌĆÖs transition team, will succeed Laurie Cumbo just four months after joining the Brooklyn nonprofit Powerhouse Arts as vice president of curatorial and arts programs.
                    
                    
                    
                        Join us today, March 2, for an exclusive, virtual talk with artist and┬ĀHyperallergic┬Ācontributor┬ĀDamien Davis, whose distinctive voice challenges the power structures and inequities of the contemporary art world.
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Affordable Art Fair New York Spring 2026Affordable Art Fair returns to the Starrett-Lehigh building for an incredible showcase of 90 galleries presenting thousands of one-of-a-kind artworks, all priced from $100 to $12,000. From March 18ŌĆō22, interact with brilliantly curated installations, enjoy food and drinks, immerse yourself in artworks from all over the world, and fall in love with collecting art.┬Ā
                        
                        
                            Get tickets
                        
                    
            OpinionArt Not Genocide Alliance protesting outside the Israeli and United States pavilions at the 2024 Venice Biennale (photo by Matteo de Mayda, courtesy Art Not Genocide Alliance)The Case for Palestine at the Venice BiennaleDoes the exhibition prefer to destroy its long-standing significance for artists, curators, and art workers than to hold the moral clarity that this moment is asking from us? | Art Not Genocide AllianceDispatch From FriezeAmanda Ross-Ho, ŌĆ£Untitled Orbit (MANUAL MODE)ŌĆØ (2026) (photo Matt Stromberg/Hyperallergic)Frieze LA Meets the Real WorldCognitive dissonance can be hard to shake at any art fair, let alone Frieze Los Angeles. This year, though, Matt Stromberg reports on several moments of quiet resistance that made the way artists engage with contemporary issues impossible to ignore. These range from artworks themselves ŌĆö including Y. Malik Jalal's embedded car-mat pieces and Christina Fernandez's photographs ŌĆö to a conflict between the fair and a nonprofit that works with migrant communities along the US-Mexico border.From Our CriticsLouis Armstrong, Basin Street East, New York (c. 1954ŌĆō56) (┬® Lisette Model Foundation; image courtesy Eakins Press Foundation/The Metropolitan Museum of Art via Art Resource, New York)The Jazz Pictures the FBI SilencedFearing for her safety, Lisette Model buried her photos of artists like Billie Holiday and Louis Armstrong, but a new book reveals them to the world. | Julia Curl
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            IDSVA Extends PhD Program to BA and BFA ApplicantsEarn your PhD in Art Theory, Aesthetics, and Philosophy with the Institute for Doctoral Studies in the Visual Arts (IDSVA).
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            Political AmnesiaThe abandoned Museo de Memoria de Colombia in Bogot├Ī (all photos Kurt Hollander/Hyperallergic, unless otherwise noted)The Uncertain Future of ColombiaŌĆÖs Museum of MemoryIt's been 15 years since the government greenlit the creation of the Museo de Memoria de Colombia in Bogot├Ī, a space to collectively remember, grieve, and learn. But as Kurt Hollander reports, the Brutalist fortress of a building now sits abandoned after mismanagement and interference from the political right, who prefer to leave the Colombian government's role in the 70-year armed conflict out of the history books (and the museums).A Book of the Dead in BrooklynDetail from an illustrated Book of the Dead (c. 305ŌĆō30 BCE), papyrus, ink, gold, and paper (photo courtesy the Brooklyn Museum)Finding God at the Brooklyn MuseumGreta Rainbow spoke to superstar conservator Ahmed Tarek, from the Grand Egyptian Museum in Cairo, about what it was like to restore the more than 2,000-year-old Egyptian Books of the Dead, now on view at the Brooklyn Museum. ŌĆ£You just have to cut your breath and be very, very patient,ŌĆØ he told her. ŌĆ£Every day for 18 months, we went to work and peeled acidic paper from just this one object.ŌĆØ The end result is breathtaking, inducing a real sense of reverence for our species, the past, and the people who preserve it.From the Archive Creative TimeŌĆÖs Diya Vij Helps Launch an Art World Think TankThe unorthodox program will gather together a group of thought leaders for 10 months to reflect and advise on happenings in the art community. | Hrag Vartanian
                  
                      
                        Daily Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: Curator Diya Vij Picked as New York CityŌĆÖs Next Commissioner of Cultural Affairs
description: Diya Vij, a curator and current vice president of curatorial and arts programmes at Powerhouse Arts, has been picked to be New York CityŌĆÖs next Depa
body:
Diya Vij, a curator and current vice president of curatorial and arts programmes at Powerhouse Arts, has been picked to be New York CityŌĆÖs next Department of Cultural Affairs (DCA) commissioner, sources with knowledge of the pick told ARTnews. The New York Times confirmed the news on Saturday.

	Considered to be one of the most important jobs in the cityŌĆÖs arts ecosystem, the commissioner is a hotly watched role whenever a new mayor enters office. The DCA is the largest municipal funder of the arts in the US and provides funding to over 800 cultural organizations throughout the cityŌĆÖs five boroughs. Last fiscal year, the DCA provided $245 million in funding. Naturally, the ascension of Mayor Zohran Mamdani, whose wife, Rama Duwaji, is an artist, has intensified specualtion on who would be picked.
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Mamdani described Vij in a statement to the Times as a ŌĆ£visionary and deeply thoughtful leader who understands that art is not ornamental to this city ŌĆö it is essential to it.ŌĆØ

	ŌĆ£Under DiyaŌĆÖs leadership, we will fight to keep New York a city where artists can afford to live and create, and where every New Yorker, in every borough, can experience the energy and inspiration that makes art possible,ŌĆØ the statement reads.

	Vij, 40, will report to Julie Su, the cityŌĆÖs first deputy mayor for economic justice. Vij is the first person of South Asian descent to hold the commissioner role.

	In November, Mamdani kicked off the speculation when he named the members of his arts and culture transition committees, a 28-member group that included art dealers, curators, journalists, and arts nonprofit administrators. Among those selected were curator and writer Kimberly Drew; Ruba Katrib, chief curator and director of curatorial affairs at MoMA PS1; Legacy Russell, executive director and chief curator at the Kitchen; and Gonzalo Casals, a former commissioner of cultural affairs for New York City.

	Also on the committee was Vij, who was appointed to her position at Powerhouse, the Brooklyn-bsased hub for arts fabrication and programming, in November. It turns out her tenure at Powerhouse was very short-lived. Prior to Powerhouse, Vij served as a curator at Creative Time where she launched CTHQ, a gathering space for artists working at the intersection of art and politics; established a fellowship for socially engaged artists; relaunched the Creative Time Summit; and helped realize several public art commissions. She previously served as an associate curator of public programs at the High Line.
	

	Vij has previously worked in the DCA for four and a half years, from 2014 to 2019 under De Blasio appointee Tom Finkelpearl, as a digital communications manager. She also launched and managed DCAŌĆÖs Public Artists in Residence program and led the agencyŌĆÖs Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion initiative. She started her career at the Queens Museum, at the time also under FinkelpearlŌĆÖs leadership, first as a curatorial fellow and then in digital communications.

	Vij enters the office at a turbulent period for the arts. An unstable economy and rising costsŌĆöboth in the New York and across the USŌĆöhas forced many galleries to close and artists to leave New York, while the Trump Administration has cut grants and funding to arts organizations and attempted to punish museums who do not follow his administrationŌĆÖs directives on ideology and eliminating Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion initiatives. As Vij told the Times, another wrinkle is the lingering effects of the pandemic.

	ŌĆ£We see how our organizations have to contract because funding is contracting,ŌĆØ she said, ŌĆ£because thereŌĆÖs an uneven Covid recovery, because thereŌĆÖs political intimidation coming from the federal government and the right.ŌĆØ

	She continued, ŌĆ£All this instability creates a landscape of fear that makes it really difficult for organizations to take the risks that they need to take, to address the issues of the day and be spaces for community to come together in joy and imagination and dissent.ŌĆØ
	

	Throughout her career, Vij has championed art that is both challenging and politically engaged. Among the projects she worked on was The WorldŌĆÖs UnFair, a heralded 2023 installation in Long Island City commissioned by Creative Time. That piece, by New Red Order, an indigenous artist collective, called for the return of public and private lands to Indigenous people. 

	ŌĆ£IŌĆÖm excited to apply my political lens to strengthening the systems that makes open, accessible and sometimes radical cultural activities possible,ŌĆØ Vij said.

schema.headline: Curator Diya Vij Picked as New York CityŌĆÖs Next Commissioner of Cultural Affairs
